thread.title: Michigan City historic church receives 'order of condemnation' over safety concerns
thread.title_full: Michigan City historic church receives 'order of condemnation' over safety concerns

author: Tyonna Baxter, WSBT 22 Reporter
title: Michigan City historic church receives 'order of condemnation' over safety concerns
text: MICHIGAN CITY, Ind. (WSBT) — A historic church in Michigan City is at the center of a debate over safety versus preservation. The city has raised concerns over the deterioration of God’s Grace Church’s, while the pastor and congregation are pleading for more time to save it. Standing for over a century, the building housing God’s Grace Church has been a part of Michigan City’s history. But now concerns over crumbling walls have placed it at risk of closure. Doors that were open for many are now closed, and the future of the church is in question. It’s been kind of in a rougher shape for the past probably maybe 8 to 10 years we have seen the change in the church,” said Kathryn & Bruce Stransky, who live next door to the church. Once a thriving congregation, the church now faces declining attendance and significant structural issues. Two long-time residents want to see the building preserved not demolished and would be sad to see it go. You know be maintained as a church maybe not as active as it once was, but it is part of the history of this neighborhood,” said the Stranskys. But with cracks in the walls and structural concerns growing, the City has stepped in, putting barricades around the church and cutting off utilities to prevent potential accidents. This is something the Stranskys want to avoid as well, especially with it being across the street from a school. Walking past the church that it doesn’t decide that this is the moment that someone or child going to school ends up in the rubble. That’s our biggest concern. I don’t wanna steal it for them. I just want it to be safe for everyone,” said the Stranskys. The City served the church an "order of condemnation" on November 20th, citing severe damage, safety risks, and unsanitary conditions that pose a hazard to public health and safety. Neighbors said the church's interim pastor is pleading with the city to allow more time to address the building’s issues. But time is not on the church's side as the city has set a deadline. Michigan City Spokesperson Kelley Smith said in a statement: They have 10 days from the date they were served the notice to request an appeal hearing before the Michigan City Board of Public Works and Safety. With an administrative hearing potentially pending, the City does not wish to comment on the matter further at this time. WSBT 22 News was unable to get in contact with church officials. City officials said they’re willing to work with the church to avoid closure, but the clock is ticking. For now, the church is racing against time to save a piece of history.
